title: Control Flow
summary: Make decisions in your code using if, else if, and else statements

sections.title: Control Flow as a Fork in the Road
sections.content: Life is full of decisions: if it's raining, take an umbrella; else, wear sunglasses. Java programs make similar decisions using control flow statements. The if-else structure is like arriving at a fork in a hiking trail. You check a condition — is the left trail marked 'summit'? If yes, go left. If no, go right. Your program evaluates a boolean condition (true or false) and takes one path or the other. This allows your program to react differently based on data or user input.

sections.title: Grade Checker Using if-else if-else
sections.content: This code assigns a letter grade based on a numerical exam score

sections.title: Boolean Expressions and Logical Operators
sections.content: The conditions inside if statements are boolean expressions — they always evaluate to true or false. You can combine multiple conditions using logical operators. AND (&&) requires both conditions to be true — like needing both a ticket AND a reservation to board a flight. OR (||) requires at least one condition to be true — like needing a passport OR a driver's license to enter a building. NOT (!) flips true to false and vice versa. These let you express complex rules with simple building blocks.

sections.title: Using = instead of == in conditions
sections.content: Most common mistake: if (age = 18) instead of if (age == 18). The single equals sign assigns a value, it does not compare. Java will not warn you about this if the variable is a boolean, but for numbers it will cause a compilation error. Fix: always use == for comparison. Wrong: if (score = 100) { ... }. Correct: if (score == 100) { ... }.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: A movie theater charges different prices: seniors (65+) pay $7, adults (18-64) pay $12, children (3-17) pay $8, and babies (under 3) are free. Write code that takes an age variable and prints the correct ticket price.
hints: Which age range should you check first to avoid overlap?
How many else-if branches do you need?
What operator checks if a value falls between two numbers?
